annotations.text: The differential diagnosis of the hepatic lesion is long, because the imaging characteristics are not clear-cut.
The lesion was 8 cm in diameter and presumably slow-growing, since the patient had had pain in this area for four years.
Furthermore, the loss of weight makes me consider the lesion a cancer until it has been proved otherwise.
These characteristics are reminiscent of a hemangioma, perhaps a cavernous hemangioma.
if it had been involved, the patient would probably have had symptoms of gastric-outlet obstruction.
Since the intensity of this lesion appears to be similar to that of the surrounding liver, it is probably not a giant cavernous hemangioma.
Other benign lesions that should be considered in this case are focal nodular hyperplasia and hepatic adenoma.
The absence of an appropriate history and the presence of a scar or necrosis on images of this patient's lesion suggest that it was not a hepatic adenoma.
Focal nodular hyperplasia is uncommon; its frequency is highest in the third to fifth decades of life, with a preponderance of cases in women.
Although focal nodular hyperplasia is slow-growing and fits many of the findings of the imaging studies in this case, it is difficult to understand how it would lead to the weight loss.
Among malignant tumors, hepatocellular carcinoma, its fibrolamellar variant, peripheral cholangiocarcinoma, angiosarcoma, and primary hepatic lymphoma are the most likely possibilities.
Since this patient's lesion was enhanced, albeit slowly, and was isodense later, it was probably not a lymphoma.

Angiosarcoma is a rare malignant tumor that is hypervascular. However, it is usually characterized by greater heterogeneity on CT and MRI scanning than was present in this case and has a more rapid course.
fibrolamellar carcinoma occurs most often in young patients, predominantly females. It is often slow-growing, and a cure is possible if the lesion is widely resected.
The description of the skin lesions suggests psoriasis, which is often confined to the hands and elbows and is characterized by scaling plaques.
Since the patient under discussion had no history of taking home remedies for her skin lesions, however, it is unlikely that she had been exposed to arsenic.
In summary, I favor the diagnosis of fibrolamellar hepatocellular carcinoma.

Complete resection is possible in 48 to 75 percent of patients with this type of hepatocellular carcinoma, as compared with a resectability rate of approximately 20 percent among patients with other types.
